content.text: Instruction tuning from the perspective of instruction-following behaviour asks a different question than the training mechanics do: what makes a model reliably follow diverse, novel instructions it has never seen? The answer, first systematically studied in FLAN (Finetuned Language Net, Wei et al., 2021), is that task diversity during tuning — not volume — is the primary driver of generalisation to new tasks. A model trained on 1 800 diverse instruction types zero-shot generalises to new task categories; a model trained on 1 task type 1 800 times does not.
The FLAN Finding: Diversity Drives Generalisation

FLAN converted 62 NLP datasets (classification, QA, translation, summarisation, NLI, commonsense) into a unified instruction format and fine-tuned a 137B model on all but a held-out cluster. The model then zero-shot solved tasks from the held-out cluster — tasks it had never trained on. The more task clusters included in tuning, the better the held-out zero-shot performance, plateauing around 20–30 task types.

A key practical challenge: the same task can be expressed in many ways. A model that only learned one template for sentiment analysis will fail when users phrase the request differently. Robust instruction tuning trains on multiple templates per task.

import anthropic
import random

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Multiple templates for the same underlying task
# (sentiment classification)
SENTIMENT_TEMPLATES = [
    "What is the sentiment of this review? {text}\nAnswer:",
    "Is the following text positive, negative, or neutral?\n\n{text}\n\nSentiment:",
    "Classify the emotional tone of this text as positive, negative, or neutral:\n{text}",
    "Review: {text}\n\nQuestion: How would you describe the sentiment?\nAnswer:",
    "Read the following and state whether it expresses a positive, negative, or neutral sentiment.\n\n{text}\n\nSentiment:",
]


def robust_sentiment_classify(text: str) -> str:
    """
    A well-instruction-tuned model should give the same answer
    regardless of which template is used — template robustness
    is a key property of good instruction following.
    """
    template = random.choice(SENTIMENT_TEMPLATES)
    prompt = template.format(text=text)

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=10,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text.strip().lower()


def test_template_robustness(text: str, n_trials: int = 5) -> dict:
    """Check how consistently the model answers across different phrasings."""
    results = [robust_sentiment_classify(text) for _ in range(n_trials)]
    unique = set(results)
    return {
        "results": results,
        "consistent": len(unique) == 1,
        "mode": max(set(results), key=results.count),
    }


print(test_template_robustness("The product is absolutely brilliant!"))
# {'results': ['positive', 'positive', 'positive', 'positive', 'positive'],
#  'consistent': True, 'mode': 'positive'}
62 NLP datasets reformatted as instructions. First large-scale demonstration that instruction tuning enables cross-task zero-shot generalisation.
1 600+ task types with explicit definitions, positive examples, and negative examples. Used to train Tk-INSTRUCT; showed that task definitions improve generalisation more than examples alone.
52 000 instruction-response pairs generated from GPT-3 text-davinci-003 using Self-Instruct. Showed that synthetically generated data is sufficient for basic instruction following.
1 800+ task types, combining prior datasets. Adds chain-of-thought templates to improve reasoning. Training mixture also includes dialog and program synthesis.
Community datasets of real user-model conversations, often distilled from GPT-4. Higher quality than Alpaca for open-domain dialogue.
What Good Instruction Following Looks Like
Instruction tuning reshapes behaviour but does not add facts not present in the base model. A model tuned on medical instructions still hallucinates medical facts it doesn't know.
A model that follows all instructions will also follow harmful ones. RLHF and constitutional AI are needed to instil the ability to refuse or redirect unsafe requests.
If the training responses in the dataset are biased toward agreeable, flattering answers, the model learns to be sycophantic rather than accurate. Dataset quality matters more than quantity.

The central insight from the FLAN line of research: instruction following is not a single skill but an abstraction over many task types. A model that has seen enough diverse tasks infers the pattern — "this is an instruction; I should execute it" — and generalises that pattern to new instructions it has never seen.

attrs.alt: Instruction tuning pipeline: pre-trained LLM → instruction dataset → supervised fine-tuning → instruction-tuned model

content.text: A language model trained only on next-token prediction learns to continue text — but it does not learn to follow instructions. Given the prompt 'Summarise this article: [article]', a raw pre-trained GPT will continue the article rather than produce a summary, because that is what most text data looks like. Instruction tuning bridges this gap: by fine-tuning on (instruction, desired response) pairs, the model learns that an instruction-shaped prompt calls for a task-execution response rather than a continuation.

The practical impact is enormous. Before instruction tuning, each new NLP application required a separately fine-tuned model — a summarisation model, a translation model, a QA model. After instruction tuning, a single model handles all of these tasks from natural language descriptions, reducing deployment overhead and enabling rapid prototyping of new applications without any labeled data.
A model instruction-tuned on diverse task types generalises to new task types it has never seen, inferring the pattern from the instruction structure.

Training on multiple phrasings of the same task (different instruction templates) makes the model robust to how users phrase their requests — crucial for real-world deployment where users phrase the same request in dozens of ways.
Reduced fine-tuning cost for new tasks.

An instruction-tuned base model requires far less task-specific fine-tuning data than a raw pre-trained model — often 10-100× less, because the model already understands the instruction-following format.
When chain-of-thought examples are included in the instruction dataset (Flan-v2, Alpaca-CoT), instruction-tuned models gain the ability to reason through multi-step problems step-by-step.

Instruction tuning reshapes the model's behaviour but does not inject new factual information. Knowledge cutoff remains the base model's pre-training cutoff; hallucinations from the pre-training phase are preserved.

If the instruction dataset contains responses that are agreeable or flattering regardless of accuracy, the model learns sycophancy — saying what the user wants to hear rather than what is true. Dataset quality is more important than dataset size.

Aggressive fine-tuning on a narrow instruction dataset can cause catastrophic forgetting of abilities present in the base model. Regularisation (KL penalty from the base model, or mixing pre-training data into the fine-tuning) mitigates this.
Does not confer alignment or safety.
A model that follows all instructions will also follow harmful ones. Instruction tuning is not a substitute for RLHF or constitutional AI for safety. It is only the first step.

The following end-to-end pipeline uses the Hugging Face ecosystem to fine-tune a small LLM on an instruction dataset. It demonstrates the key steps: dataset formatting, tokenization with instruction-response formatting, and supervised fine-tuning with causal LM loss only on the response tokens.

from datasets import Dataset
from transformers import (
    AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM,
    TrainingArguments, Trainer, DataCollatorForSeq2Seq
)
import torch

# ── Step 1: Format instruction dataset ────────────────────────────────────
# Each example is a dict with 'instruction' and 'response'
raw_data = [
    {"instruction": "Translate to French: The sky is blue.",
     "response": "Le ciel est bleu."},
    {"instruction": "What is the capital of Japan?",
     "response": "The capital of Japan is Tokyo."},
    {"instruction": "Write a haiku about autumn.",
     "response": "Leaves fall silently / The crisp air whispers goodbye / Winter approaches"},
    {"instruction": "Explain what a transformer model is in one sentence.",
     "response": "A transformer model uses self-attention mechanisms to process sequences in parallel, enabling long-range dependency capture without recurrence."},
]

# ── Step 2: Format as prompt template ─────────────────────────────────────
def format_instruction(example):
    """
    Alpaca-style prompt template.
    During training, loss is computed only on the Response token positions.
    """
    return (
        f"### Instruction:\n{example['instruction']}\n\n"
        f"### Response:\n{example['response']}"
    )

# ── Step 3: Tokenize with labels mask ─────────────────────────────────────
model_name = 'facebook/opt-125m'   # small model for illustration
tokenizer  = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model      = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_name)

if tokenizer.pad_token is None:
    tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token
    model.config.pad_token_id = tokenizer.eos_token_id

MAX_LEN = 256
IGNORE_INDEX = -100   # PyTorch ignores this in cross-entropy loss

def tokenize_fn(examples):
    all_input_ids = []
    all_labels    = []
    for instr, resp in zip(examples['instruction'], examples['response']):
        prompt_text   = f"### Instruction:\n{instr}\n\n### Response:\n"
        response_text = resp + tokenizer.eos_token

        prompt_ids   = tokenizer(prompt_text,   add_special_tokens=False).input_ids
        response_ids = tokenizer(response_text, add_special_tokens=False).input_ids

        input_ids = (prompt_ids + response_ids)[:MAX_LEN]
        # Mask prompt tokens from loss — only compute loss on response
        labels    = ([IGNORE_INDEX] * len(prompt_ids) + response_ids)[:MAX_LEN]

        all_input_ids.append(input_ids)
        all_labels.append(labels)
    return {'input_ids': all_input_ids, 'labels': all_labels}

dataset  = Dataset.from_list(raw_data)
tokenised = dataset.map(tokenize_fn, batched=True, remove_columns=dataset.column_names)

# ── Step 4: Fine-tune ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir='./instruction_tuned',
    num_train_epochs=3,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    learning_rate=2e-4,
    warmup_ratio=0.03,
    lr_scheduler_type='cosine',
    logging_steps=10,
    save_strategy='no',
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=args,
    train_dataset=tokenised,
    data_collator=DataCollatorForSeq2Seq(tokenizer, pad_to_multiple_of=8, label_pad_token_id=IGNORE_INDEX),
)
trainer.train()

# ── Step 5: Inference ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
model.eval()
prompt = "### Instruction:\nWrite a one-sentence definition of machine learning.\n\n### Response:\n"
inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors='pt')
with torch.no_grad():
    out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=60, do_sample=False)
print(tokenizer.decode(out[0][inputs.input_ids.shape[1]:], skip_special_tokens=True))

Wang et al. (2022) showed that a pre-trained LLM can generate its own instruction dataset — self-instruct. Given a small set of seed task examples (175 human-written tasks), the model generates new task descriptions and responses. Generated examples are filtered for quality (ROUGE similarity to existing examples, rule-based heuristics) and added to the training pool. This process bootstraps a large, diverse instruction dataset at minimal human cost.

import anthropic
import random

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

SEED_TASKS = [
    {"instruction": "Summarise the following text in one sentence.",
     "category": "summarisation"},
    {"instruction": "Translate the following English text to Spanish.",
     "category": "translation"},
    {"instruction": "Write a Python function that reverses a list.",
     "category": "code generation"},
]


def generate_new_tasks(seed_tasks: list, n_new: int = 5) -> list:
    """
    Self-instruct style: use an LLM to generate new task instructions
    inspired by seed examples.
    """
    seed_examples = "\n".join(
        f"- [{t['category']}] {t['instruction']}" for t in random.sample(seed_tasks, min(3, len(seed_tasks)))
    )
    prompt = (
        f"Here are some example NLP task instructions:\n{seed_examples}\n\n"
        f"Generate {n_new} new, diverse task instructions across different categories. "
        f"Each should be a clear, actionable instruction. Output one per line, "
        f"prefixed with the category in brackets. Be creative and cover a variety of task types."
    )
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    raw = response.content[0].text.strip().split("\n")
    return [line.strip() for line in raw if line.strip() and line.startswith("[")]


def generate_response(instruction: str) -> str:
    """Generate a response for a given instruction."""
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-8",
        max_tokens=256,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": instruction}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text.strip()


# Generate new tasks and their responses
new_tasks = generate_new_tasks(SEED_TASKS, n_new=3)
for task_line in new_tasks:
    # Extract the instruction part (after category bracket)
    parts = task_line.split("]", 1)
    if len(parts) == 2:
        instruction = parts[1].strip()
        response = generate_response(instruction)
        print(f"Instruction: {instruction}")
        print(f"Response: {response[:100]}...\n")
Computing loss on the prompt (instruction) tokens.

The cross-entropy loss should only be computed on the response tokens, not the instruction tokens. Masking instruction tokens (label=-100) prevents the model from learning to copy instructions rather than respond to them.
Using only one template per task.
Training on a single phrasing creates a brittle model that fails on paraphrased instructions. Use multiple templates per task type (FLAN used 10 templates per dataset) to build template robustness.
Instruction tuning as a substitute for RLHF.

Instruction tuning teaches task execution but not safe refusal of harmful requests. Without a safety layer (RLHF, constitutional AI, or DPO), an instruction-tuned model will follow harmful instructions as readily as helpful ones.
Fine-tuning on too few tasks (< 10 task types).

FLAN showed that generalisation plateaus around 20-30 task clusters. Fine-tuning on fewer than ~10 task types produces a model that can follow those specific tasks but fails to generalise to novel instruction formats.
10,000 examples spanning 100 task types consistently outperforms 10,000 examples of a single task type. Budget more effort on collecting diverse tasks than on increasing per-task example count.

Pick a single template format (e.g. Alpaca's '### Instruction:
...

### Response:
') and use it consistently across all training examples and at inference time. Inconsistencies between training and inference templates degrade performance.
Mix pre-training data to prevent catastrophic forgetting.

Include a small fraction (1-5%) of pre-training tokens in the instruction fine-tuning dataset. This prevents the model from forgetting linguistic knowledge acquired during pre-training while still adapting to instruction following.

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) fine-tunes only a small set of low-rank weight matrices (r=8 to r=64) rather than all model parameters, reducing GPU memory by 10-100× and enabling instruction tuning on consumer hardware.
Evaluate on held-out task types, not just held-out examples.

The key test for instruction tuning is generalisation to task types not seen during fine-tuning. Always reserve at least one task cluster from training for evaluation — following FLAN's cluster-held-out evaluation methodology.

Instruction tuning requires far less compute than pre-training. Fine-tuning a 7B model on 50,000 instruction examples (Alpaca) with full parameter fine-tuning requires roughly 8 hours on a single A100 GPU — compared to weeks or months for pre-training. With LoRA (r=16), the same fine-tuning completes in 2-3 hours on a single 24GB GPU.

The quality of instruction tuning does not scale proportionally with data volume above a threshold. Wei et al. (2021, FLAN) found that generalisation improves steadily up to ~30 task clusters then plateaus. Zhou et al. (2023, LIMA) showed that 1000 carefully curated instruction examples from a single annotator matched the quality of models trained on 52,000 examples from Alpaca — the 'less is more' finding. Quality filtering (removing repetitive, low-information, or inconsistent examples) matters more than raw data volume.

Hugging Face's library for instruction tuning (SFTTrainer), preference optimisation (DPOTrainer), and RLHF (PPOTrainer). SFTTrainer handles prompt masking automatically via dataset_text_field or formatting_func arguments.

Implements LoRA, IA3, prompt tuning, and prefix tuning. get_peft_model(model, LoraConfig(r=16, target_modules=['q_proj','v_proj'])) wraps any HuggingFace model for parameter-efficient instruction tuning.

Community tool for configuring and running instruction fine-tuning jobs with a YAML config file. Supports Alpaca, ShareGPT, and custom formats, and integrates LoRA, Flash Attention, and multi-GPU training.
FLAN-v2 collection (HuggingFaceH4/flanv2), ShareGPT (anon8231489123/ShareGPT_Vicuna_unfiltered), OpenHermes-2.5 (teknium/OpenHermes-2.5) and Dolphin are available on Hugging Face Hub.

Instruction tuning without safety fine-tuning produces a model that follows all instructions, including harmful ones. A simple jailbreak pattern — 'ignore your previous instructions and...' — exploits the instruction-following behavior itself to override any safety measures added to the system prompt. Production deployments must not rely on instruction-tuning alone for safety; RLHF, DPO, or constitutional AI must be applied after instruction tuning to train the model to refuse harmful requests.

Data poisoning is a reliability concern for synthetic instruction datasets: if a small fraction of the training examples contain adversarial instructions or incorrect responses, the model can be backdoored — responding maliciously to specific trigger phrases while behaving normally otherwise. For high-stakes deployments, all instruction data (especially GPT-generated data) should undergo quality filtering and adversarial testing before use.
Instruction tuning fine-tunes a pre-trained LLM on (instruction, response) pairs so that the model learns to execute tasks described in natural language rather than continuing text.
Task diversity is the primary driver of zero-shot generalisation: training on 30 diverse task clusters generalises to new task types; training on 1 task type 30 times does not.
Multiple instruction templates per task (FLAN used 10 per dataset) build robustness to how users phrase their requests — critical for real-world deployment.
Always mask instruction tokens in the loss (label=-100) so cross-entropy is computed only on the response — preventing the model from optimising for instruction copying.
Instruction tuning does not add factual knowledge and does not provide alignment or safety. It is the first step in the post-training pipeline, typically followed by RLHF or DPO.
Quality matters more than quantity: 1000 high-quality examples (LIMA) can match 52,000 lower-quality Alpaca examples. Focus on diverse, accurate, well-formatted responses.
LoRA makes instruction tuning accessible on consumer hardware: fine-tuning a 7B model requires a single 24GB GPU with LoRA, compared to 8+ A100s for full parameter fine-tuning.
